steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what function the two underlined portions serve — ‘alabaster box’ and ‘my heart’ — in the poem as a whole. Look at how the poem uses both items across its four lines.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘They describe the subjects, one physical and one abstract, of a comparison that extends throughout the text.’: The poem opens with an explicit simile (‘Like this alabaster box... is my heart’) and sustains it through parallel descriptors applied to both: both are frail, delicate, carved with thoughts and dreams. One subject is physical (the box), one is abstract (the heart), and the comparison runs the full length of the poem. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘They symbolize two attitudes, one logical and one emotional, that are contrasted throughout the text.’: The poem never contrasts attitudes; the box and heart are linked by likeness, not opposition. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘They indicate potential reactions, one hoped for and one feared, to a decision the speaker considers throughout the text.’: There is no decision in the poem, and neither underlined phrase names a reaction. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘They provide examples, one literal and one figurative, of a change that the speaker describes throughout the text.’: No change is described; the poem is a static comparison. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
